title: SEGMENT 2 — What a phage actually is

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — What a phage actually is
So what is a bacteriophage, structurally and functionally?
At the simplest level: a phage is a virus that infects bacteria.

The word itself comes from the Greek — "phagein," to eat — so "bacteriophage" literally means "bacteria eater," and the name was coined by Félix d'Hérelle in 1917 because that's exactly what they looked like they were doing on his plates.
We'll get to him in a moment.
A phage is, like any virus, basically two things: a genome, and a protein shell that protects and delivers that genome.
The genome can be DNA or RNA, single-stranded or double-stranded, linear or circular — different phages use all combinations.
The shell, called the capsid, is built from one or a handful of repeating protein subunits, packed into a precise geometry.

For many of the phages we'll discuss today, the capsid is an icosahedron — a twenty-faced polyhedron, the most efficient shape for enclosing a given volume with identical subunits — sitting on top of a tail structure that does the work of attaching to and injecting through the bacterial cell wall.
Picture the classic textbook phage — say, T4, which infects E. coli.
T4 looks like a lunar lander.
It has a roughly icosahedral head, the capsid, containing the DNA genome — about 170 kilobases of double-stranded DNA, which is a lot for a virus, encoding around 300 proteins.
Attached to the head is a long, helical tail, like a syringe.
At the bottom of the tail is a baseplate with tail fibers sticking out — six long protein fibers that splay out and dangle.
Those fibers are the recognition apparatus.

When T4 bumps into an E. coli cell, the tail fibers bind specific molecules on the bacterial outer membrane — lipopolysaccharide, in T4's case — and once enough fibers have engaged, the baseplate reorients and the tail contracts like a syringe plunger, driving a hollow tube through the bacterial envelope and injecting the DNA.
The empty protein shell is left outside, stuck to the cell surface like a discarded delivery package.
Inside, the DNA takes over.
Not all phages look like T4.
M13, which we discussed at length in the phage display lecture, is a filamentous phage — a long, thin, floppy noodle of a particle, with single-stranded DNA inside a protein tube.
Lambda, the workhorse of molecular biology, looks more like T4 — icosahedral head with a tail — but its tail is shorter and not contractile.
MS2, an RNA phage, is just a small icosahedral shell with no tail at all.
P22, which infects Salmonella, has a short stubby tail.
The morphological diversity of phages is enormous, and it reflects the diversity of strategies they've evolved for getting into different bacterial hosts.
The other crucial feature of phages — and this is what makes them precision predators rather than broad poisons — is host range.
Each phage species recognizes a specific set of bacterial surface molecules.
T4 needs the lipopolysaccharide of certain E. coli strains.
M13 needs the F pilus.
Lambda needs a maltose transporter called LamB.
P22 needs a specific O-antigen on Salmonella.
If your bacterium doesn't display the right surface molecule, the phage can't bind, can't inject, can't infect.
That host specificity is exquisite.
A phage that beautifully kills one strain of E. coli may completely fail against a closely related strain that differs only in a single sugar on its surface lipopolysaccharide.
Pause.
This is the source of both the magic and the headache of phage therapy.
The magic: phages don't touch off-target bacteria.
The headache: matching the right phage to the right patient's bug is non-trivial.
Phages are also, by virus standards, structurally elaborate.
A T4 head is built from over a thousand protein subunits.
The tail tube alone is dozens of distinct proteins.

The assembly process is a sequence of choreographed protein-protein interactions that has to happen in a specific order — first the head shell, then the DNA is packaged into it under enormous pressure, then the tail is attached, then the tail fibers.
The whole thing is a tiny, autonomous molecular machine of extraordinary complexity.
And you can grow a milliliter of culture and harvest ten to the twelfth of them.
The plumbing of life at industrial scale.
One more thing about size, because it'll matter later.
Phage particles are small — typically 50 to 200 nanometers in their largest dimension.
That's smaller than a single ribosome by a noticeable margin, and well below the resolution of light microscopy.
You can't see phages with an ordinary microscope.
You can see what they do — the clear holes they punch in a bacterial lawn, the plaques, which we'll get to in the discovery section.
But the particles themselves are visible only by electron microscopy.

The fact that for the first thirty years of phage research nobody had ever actually seen a phage, only its effects, is part of why early phage work feels a little like astronomy — you're inferring an invisible thing from its consequences.
Alright.
We have a particle.
Now let's build the lifestyle.
